perturbation.original_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Board of Directors will determine the amount of any extra payment. It will be based on each member's fair share of the deficiency. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a payment in cash. If you do not make the extra payment within 60 days from the date we notify you of your share of the deficiency, the amount will be charged as an indebtedness against the contract with interest compounded at the rate of 5% per year.
perturbation.changed_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Board of Directors will determine if an extra payment is necessary.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the details on how the extra payment is determined, the methods of payment, and the consequences of not paying, this change introduces significant ambiguity. The omission of the penalty for non-payment creates a legal contradiction because it removes a component of the agreement, violating state contract law requiring clarity and enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 705.03, alongside established contract law principles, requires clarity in contract terms, particularly regarding obligations and remedies. Removing the details of how deficiency payments are calculated and enforced renders the contract ambiguous and potentially unenforceable, violating the spirit and letter of Wisconsin contract law. The omission significantly weakens the consumer's understanding of their financial obligations and the consequences of non-compliance.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text details the process for determining extra payments, methods of payment, and consequences of non-payment. The changed text removes these details, only stating that the Board of Directors will determine if an extra payment is necessary. The perturbation explanation states that this creates ambiguity, and the law explanation says this violates Wisconsin contract law by making the contract unenforceable. Since this falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, where a contract removes essential information, and since legally mandated consumer protection details have been omitted, this constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: DIVIDENDS. Each year, we will determine our divisible surplus. This contract's share, if any, will be credited as a dividend. Since we do not expect this contract to contribute to divisible surplus, it is not expected that any dividends will be credited. DIVIDEND OPTIONS. If dividends are credited after premiums can no longer be paid under this contract, dividends will be paid in cash. Otherwise, dividends will be applied under the Payment of Premium option unless the Cash option has been chosen in writing. Cash. Dividends are paid in cash. Payment of Premium. Dividends are applied as payment of a Net Premium.
perturbation.changed_text: DIVIDENDS. Each year, we will determine our divisible surplus. This contract's share, if any, will be credited as a dividend. Since we do not expect this contract to contribute to divisible surplus, it is not expected that any dividends will be credited.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the 'Dividend Options' section, the contract fails to specify how dividends will be handled if they are ever credited. This omission is a legal contradiction of Wisconsin insurance regulations regarding transparency and clarity in how policyholder benefits are handled, creating potential disputes and disadvantaging the consumer.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Administrative Code Ins 2.14 requires insurance policies to clearly explain how policyholder benefits, including dividends, are handled. By removing the explanation of dividend options, the contract fails to meet this requirement for clear disclosure and could mislead policyholders about their rights, creating a regulatory violation.

Rules Indices Miscellaneous Archives Home Bill, Rule, and Appointment Histories Senators Representatives Committees Text of Introduced Proposals Amendment Text Acts Veto Messages Enrolled Bills Votes Assembly and Senate Floor Calendars Schedule of Committee Activities Assembly and Senate Journals Committee Records (ROCPs) Legislative Rules All Session-Related Documents Subject Index to Acts Subject Index to Legislation Subject Index to Journals Author Index to Legislation Subject Index to Clearinghouse Rules Miscellaneous Budget Documents Executive Orders Rulings of the Chair Wisconsin Supreme Court Rules Opinions of the Attorney General Town Law Forms Law Districts Session Drafting Files Feeds Preferences Show tree Hide tree Feedback Help Home Senate Home Senators Committees Session Chief Clerk Sergeant at Arms Civics Education Human Resources Assembly Home Representatives Committees Session Chief Clerk Sergeant at Arms Human Resources Schedule Joint Senate Assembly Study Legislative Audit Bureau Legislative Council Legislative Fiscal Bureau Legislative Human Resources Office Legislative Reference Bureau Legislative Technology Services Bureau Menu » Administrative Rules Related » Administrative Code » Office of the Commissioner of Insurance (Ins) » Chapter Ins 2 Up Up Ins 2.13(9)(a) (a) Before any insurer issues any modified guaranteed life insurance policy or modified guaranteed annuity contract in this state, the commissioner may require the insurer to file a copy of any prospectus or other sales material to be used in connection with the marketing of the modified guaranteed life insurance policy or modified guaranteed annuity contract. The sales material shall clearly illustrate that there can be both upward and downward adjustments due to the application of the market-value adjustment formula in determining nonforfeiture benefits. Ins 2.13
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'Dividend Options' section contradicts the principle that insurance policies must clearly explain how policyholder benefits are handled, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly state this requirement, it implies the need for clear illustration regarding benefits by mandating the filing of sales material that illustrates adjustments in certain policies. The Perturbation Explanation strengthens this by asserting the omission creates potential disputes due to a lack of transparency regarding policyholder benefits.

perturbation.original_text: ASSIGNMENT The ASSIGNMENT section is amended to include the following: You may transfer ownership of this contract in accordance with our bylaws. Society membership rights and privileges cannot be transferred or assigned.
perturbation.changed_text: ASSIGNMENT The ASSIGNMENT section is amended to include the following: You may transfer ownership of this contract. Society membership rights and privileges cannot be transferred or assigned.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'in accordance with our bylaws,' the contract becomes vague about the specific procedures required for transferring ownership. This omission contradicts Wisconsin law requiring insurance contracts to clearly outline the terms and conditions for policy assignments, potentially leading to disputes and hindering the policyholder's ability to exercise their rights. This introduces a legal risk by failing to adhere to the state's transparency requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute § 631.21 mandates that insurance policies contain clear and complete terms. By omitting the reference to bylaws governing contract assignment, the amended text obscures the precise procedures policyholders must follow to transfer ownership, thus creating ambiguity and violating the statutory requirement for clarity.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text omits the phrase 'in accordance with our bylaws', removing essential information about the procedures for transferring ownership. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition: 'Omissions occur when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes. A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.' The Law Explanation states that Wisconsin Statute § 631.21 mandates clear and complete terms in insurance policies, and the omission obscures the precise procedures for transferring ownership. Since a key aspect of the assignment process has been removed, potentially hindering a policyholder's ability to understand their rights, a contradiction exists.
